confidence.disclosure: Documented from the Kitab-i Bahriye, the colophons of his 1513 and 1528 world maps, and Ottoman state records. The 1528-1547 'silent years' are essentially undocumented and have been left out. Birth date is approximate (between 1465 and 1470). Death date is given as 1553 by most Ottoman sources but some give 1554.

sources: Piri Reis, Kitab-i Bahriye (1521 and 1525 versions)
Piri Reis, world maps of 1513 and 1528 (Topkapı Palace, Istanbul)
Svat Soucek, Piri Reis and Turkish Mapmaking after Columbus (1996)
Cengiz Orhonlu, articles on Piri Reis in Belleten (1970s)

journey.event: Born in Gallipoli, the Ottoman naval base on the Dardanelles; nephew of the corsair Kemal Reis.
Sailed with Kemal Reis on raiding expeditions along the Spanish coast in the western Mediterranean.
Followed his uncle into formal Ottoman naval service under Sultan Bayezid II.
Took part in the naval Battle of Zonchio against the Venetians.
Fought at the Battle of Modon during the first Ottoman-Venetian war.
Captured Spanish ships off the Valencian coast; the prize included Christopher Columbus's chart of the New World, later used as a source for the 1513 map.
Returned to Gallipoli after Kemal Reis was lost at sea, and began work on his world map and Kitab-i Bahriye.
Completed the first world map in Muharram 919 AH (March 1513), drawing on twenty earlier charts including Columbus's.
Took part in Selim I's conquest of Mamluk Egypt and presented his 1513 world map to the sultan in Cairo.
Completed the first version of Kitab-i Bahriye, the Book of Navigation.
Took part in Suleiman the Magnificent's siege of Rhodes, which surrendered on 25 December 1522.
Presented the revised Kitab-i Bahriye to Suleiman the Magnificent through Grand Vizier Ibrahim Pasha.
Completed his second world map, of which only the northwest Atlantic fragment survives.
Appointed admiral of the Ottoman fleet in the Indian Ocean, with headquarters at Suez.
Recaptured Aden from the Portuguese on 26 February 1548.
Captured the Portuguese fortress of Muscat, which had been held since 1507.
Stormed the town of Hormuz but failed to take the inner Portuguese fortress and withdrew to Basra.
Left most of his fleet at Basra for repairs and sailed back to Egypt with three galleys laden with spoils.
Beheaded in Cairo around the age of 88 after refusing to support a further Persian Gulf campaign.
